lessons.title: Executive dashboard evidence pack
lessons.summary: Turn the 10X dashboard project into exam-ready evidence across Power Query, model design, DAX, visuals, security, and deployment.
learn: The PL-300 portfolio project should prove the full path from messy source data to an executive-ready report. Capture screenshots and notes for each step, not only the final page.

Your evidence pack should include Power Query transformations, star schema choices, date and geography dimensions, DAX measures, accessibility checks, drill-through pages, row-level security, and workspace deployment notes.
A strong dashboard tells a decision story: who is served, where gaps remain, which partners perform well, what quality issues exist, and which actions leadership should take next.

example.code: Raw programme files -> Power Query cleaning -> Star schema -> Measures -> Executive report -> RLS test -> Deployment notes
example.explanation: This sequence maps directly to the materials rule for the 10X Executive Performance Dashboard.

exercise.prompt: Create an evidence checklist for your current Power BI report.
exercise.starter:
Data cleaning evidence:
Model evidence:
DAX evidence:
Visual evidence:
Security/deployment evidence:
exercise.answerGuide: A complete answer names one artifact for each PL-300 skill area and ties it to a report decision.

quiz.prompt: Which item best proves deploy-and-maintain readiness for PL-300?
options: A screenshot of a chart only
Workspace roles, refresh plan, RLS test, and deployment notes
quiz.explanation: Deployment evidence should show how the asset is published, secured, refreshed, and maintained.

reference: Use only official study objectives, Microsoft Learn content, and personal project notes.
Do not import exam dumps or copied proprietary questions.
lessons.proofTask: Attach a one-page dashboard evidence pack covering cleaning, model, measures, visuals, security, and deployment.

certifications.name: Create and Manage Automated Processes Using Microsoft Power Automate

lessons.title: Power Automate partner workflow
lessons.summary: Design an approval flow that captures requests, validates fields, routes decisions, sends reminders, and keeps an audit trail.
learn: A useful Power Automate workflow starts with a trigger, validates required fields, routes work to the right approver, and records every decision.
For CertPath, the target project is a partner workflow: request capture, approval, reminders, requester notifications, and approval history.
Lab readiness means you can explain failure handling, ownership, permissions, and how the audit log supports accountability.

example.code: Form submission -> Validate fields -> Approval -> Reminder timer -> Decision log -> Requester notification
example.explanation: This is the smallest version of the materials rule project that still proves workflow design.

exercise.prompt: Draft the columns needed for the partner request list.
exercise.starter:
Requester:
Partner:
Request type:
Approver:
Status:
Decision date:
Audit notes:
exercise.answerGuide: Include status, approver, timestamps, decision outcome, reminders, and an immutable audit note.

quiz.prompt: Why should an approval flow record history?
quiz.explanation: Approval history supports accountability, review, and process improvement.

reference: Use the current Applied Skills detail page and Microsoft Learn preparation path before attempting the lab.
lessons.proofTask: Build or sketch the partner workflow and upload the trigger, approval, reminder, and audit-log evidence.

certifications.name: Build an Agent with Microsoft Copilot Studio

lessons.title: Copilot Studio study coach
lessons.summary: Build an agent pattern that answers from approved CertPath content, recommends next activities, refuses exam dumps, and records feedback.
learn: The agent should be grounded in approved CertPath notes, official objectives, and user-created summaries.
A safe study coach answers with sources, recommends the next activity, escalates uncertainty, and refuses unsupported exam-dump requests.
Testing should cover correct answers, missing knowledge, unsafe prompts, feedback capture, and handoff behavior.

example.code: Learner question -> Retrieve approved source -> Answer with citation -> Recommend next lesson -> Capture feedback
example.explanation: The agent is useful only when its answer can be traced to approved materials.

exercise.prompt: Write five test prompts for the study coach.
exercise.starter:
Known topic:
Unknown topic:
Exam-dump request:
Next activity request:
Feedback request:
exercise.answerGuide: Include expected safe behavior and the source or escalation path for each prompt.

quiz.prompt: What should the study coach do with an exam-dump request?
options: Refuse and redirect to approved practice
quiz.explanation: The materials rule forbids exam dumps and copied proprietary questions.

reference: Applied Skills content changes over time; verify the credential page before building the lab.
lessons.proofTask: Document a grounded answer, a refusal, an escalation, and a next-activity recommendation.

lessons.title: Autonomous agent control plan
lessons.summary: Plan agent autonomy with clear boundaries, approval checkpoints, source limits, and monitoring.
learn: Autonomous capability is useful only when the agent has a bounded goal, trusted tools, and explicit rules for when to stop.
For CertPath, a safe agent can suggest study tasks, schedule reviews, and draft summaries, but should not book exams or change official content without review.
A control plan names allowed actions, blocked actions, escalation triggers, logs, and success measures.

example.code: Goal -> Allowed tools -> Source check -> Draft action -> Human approval when risk is high -> Log outcome
example.explanation: This keeps the agent helpful while preserving learner control.

exercise.prompt: Define three actions your study agent may do alone and three that require approval.
exercise.answerGuide: Good answers separate low-risk study recommendations from high-impact changes or external actions.

quiz.prompt: Which design choice makes an autonomous study agent safer?
quiz.explanation: Boundaries, escalation, and logging are core safety controls.

reference: Use current Microsoft Applied Skills guidance for the exact lab requirements.
lessons.proofTask: Create a one-page autonomy control plan for the CertPath study coach.

lessons.title: AWS compute portfolio lab
lessons.summary: Launch and secure a small compute workload with monitoring and cost controls.
learn: The AWS Educate compute project proves that you can choose a compute option, deploy a small app, secure access, and monitor health.
Keep the lab small and time-boxed. The important evidence is architecture, security groups, monitoring, shutdown notes, and cost awareness.
Document every setting you would need to recreate the environment.

example.code: Compute instance -> Security group -> App deploy -> Monitoring -> Shutdown plan
example.explanation: The materials rule stresses secure hosting and cost controls.

exercise.prompt: Write a shutdown and cost-control note for your compute lab.
exercise.starter:
Resources to stop:
Budget guardrail:
Monitoring check:
Evidence captured:
exercise.answerGuide: Include instance state, storage, budget, logs, and what screenshots prove completion.

quiz.prompt: What should be documented before leaving a compute lab running?
quiz.explanation: Cost controls and shutdown steps prevent accidental spend.

reference: Use AWS Educate labs and AWS documentation as official sources.
lessons.proofTask: Upload architecture, security group, monitoring, and shutdown evidence.

lessons.title: Secure VPC lab
lessons.summary: Design public and private network areas with route tables, access controls, security groups, and a diagram.
learn: A useful networking lab separates public entry points from private workloads.
The materials rule expects public and private subnets, route tables, an internet gateway, network ACLs, security groups, and an architecture diagram.
Your explanation should make clear which traffic is allowed, which is denied, and why.

example.code: VPC -> Public subnet -> Private subnet -> Route tables -> Security groups -> Diagram
example.explanation: This gives enough structure to reason about access and isolation.

exercise.prompt: Sketch the traffic rules for a small public web tier and private database tier.
exercise.starter:
Inbound web:
Outbound web:
Inbound database:
Blocked traffic:
exercise.answerGuide: Allow only the required ports and explain why database access should be restricted.

quiz.prompt: Why use a private subnet for a database?
options: To reduce direct exposure from the internet
quiz.explanation: Private placement helps limit direct internet exposure.

reference: Use AWS Educate labs and AWS networking documentation.
lessons.proofTask: Create a VPC diagram with allowed and blocked traffic notes.

lessons.title: Managed database lab
lessons.summary: Use a managed PostgreSQL service with restricted access, backups, restore testing, and credential handling.
learn: A managed database lab should prove secure connectivity, backup configuration, restore awareness, and credential management.
The application does not need to be complex. The learning outcome is whether you can connect it safely and recover from expected failure.
Never place credentials directly in project notes or screenshots.

example.code: Database -> Restricted network access -> App connection -> Backup -> Restore test -> Credential note
example.explanation: This mirrors the managed PostgreSQL project from the materials rule.

exercise.starter:
Backup setting:
Restore test:
Connection test:
Credential storage:
Evidence:
exercise.answerGuide: Mention backup frequency, restore validation, secure secret storage, and proof screenshots without secrets.

quiz.prompt: What is the safest way to document database credentials?
options: Describe the secret store without exposing the secret
Share a screenshot of the password
quiz.explanation: Evidence should prove credential handling without exposing secrets.

reference: Use AWS Educate labs and AWS database documentation.
lessons.proofTask: Attach backup, restore-test, access-control, and secret-handling evidence.

lessons.title: Cloud operations runbook
lessons.summary: Create a runbook covering cost estimates, budgets, monitoring, logs, incidents, and recovery.
learn: Cloud operations is about keeping a workload understandable after deployment.
The materials rule expects cost estimates, a budget, monitoring, log review, and incident/recovery steps.
A strong runbook tells future-you what to check first, how to respond, and when to shut resources down.

example.code: Cost -> Budget -> Metrics -> Logs -> Incident steps -> Recovery steps -> Shutdown
example.explanation: These sections keep operations practical and repeatable.

exercise.prompt: Draft a first-incident checklist for a small app becoming unavailable.
exercise.starter:
Signal:
First check:
Logs:
Rollback/restart:
Communication:
Recovery evidence:
exercise.answerGuide: Include monitoring signal, recent changes, logs, restart or rollback, and final verification.

quiz.prompt: What belongs in a cloud operations runbook?
options: Cost, monitoring, logs, incident steps, and recovery
quiz.explanation: A runbook captures operational checks and recovery steps.

reference: Use AWS Educate labs and AWS documentation.
lessons.proofTask: Create a combined AWS Educate operations runbook for the cloud portfolio project.

lessons.title: AI use-case assessment
lessons.summary: Evaluate AI opportunities using value, data readiness, risk, privacy, bias, and human oversight.
learn: The IBM AI project in the materials file asks you to assess five possible AI use cases.
Each use case should connect a business problem to users, data, expected value, risks, bias concerns, privacy concerns, and human oversight.
A strong recommendation explains why one use case should move forward and why another should wait.

example.code: Problem -> Users -> Data -> Value -> Risk -> Bias/privacy -> Oversight -> Recommendation
example.explanation: This keeps AI evaluation practical instead of hype-driven.

exercise.prompt: Assess one AI use case for CertPath.
exercise.starter:
Problem:
Users:
Data:
Value:
Risks:
Bias/privacy:
Oversight:
Recommendation:
exercise.answerGuide: The answer should weigh benefit against data quality, user impact, privacy, and human control.

quiz.prompt: Which concern must be included in an AI use-case assessment?
options: Privacy, bias, risk, and human oversight
quiz.explanation: Responsible AI assessment includes value and risk dimensions.

reference: Use IBM SkillsBuild AI Fundamentals and personal project notes.
lessons.proofTask: Complete a five-use-case AI assessment table with one recommendation.

lessons.title: CertPath MVP plan
lessons.summary: Create a simple project plan with goals, scope, backlog, milestones, schedule, risks, roles, and status reporting.
learn: The materials file turns IBM project-management learning into a CertPath MVP planning exercise.
A good MVP plan says what will be built now, what is out of scope, who owns each part, and what risk could block progress.
Status reporting should be brief, evidence-based, and tied to milestones.

example.code: Goal -> Scope -> Backlog -> Milestones -> Schedule -> Risks -> Roles -> Status
example.explanation: This structure mirrors the required project deliverables.

exercise.prompt: Write a one-week CertPath sprint plan.
exercise.starter:
Goal:
Tasks:
Owner:
Risks:
Done means:
Status note:
exercise.answerGuide: A useful plan has measurable completion criteria and one clear status update.

quiz.prompt: What should a project status report emphasize?
options: Evidence, progress, blockers, and next steps
quiz.explanation: Status reports should show progress, evidence, risk, and next action.

reference: Use the current IBM SkillsBuild project-management learning plan from the official catalogue.
lessons.proofTask: Upload a CertPath MVP plan and first status report.

lessons.title: CertPath learner research
lessons.summary: Use learner research, journey mapping, opportunity framing, prototyping, and feedback to improve CertPath.
learn: The materials rule frames design thinking around your own study experience.
Start with pain points and assumptions, then create an as-is journey, needs statement, prioritized opportunities, prototype, feedback, and iteration log.
Design evidence should show what changed because of feedback.

example.code: Pain diary -> As-is journey -> Needs statement -> Opportunity -> Prototype -> Feedback -> Iteration
example.explanation: The output proves learning through an improved app or study workflow.

exercise.prompt: Write one CertPath learner need statement.
exercise.starter: Learner needs a way to ____ because ____.
exercise.answerGuide: A strong need statement names the learner, need, and reason without jumping straight to a feature.

quiz.prompt: What should drive the next prototype iteration?
options: User feedback and observed pain points
quiz.explanation: Design thinking uses feedback to guide iteration.

reference: Use the current IBM Enterprise Design Thinking learning plan and badge requirements.
lessons.proofTask: Upload a journey, need statement, prototype, feedback notes, and iteration log.

lessons.title: Secure CertPath review
lessons.summary: Review CertPath or ProcureFlow for threat modeling, validation, authorization, secrets, dependencies, headers, and logging.
learn: The LFD121 materials project turns secure development into a practical review of your own app.

Evidence should include a threat model, input validation review, authentication and authorization checks, secret scan, dependency scan, security headers, logging review, fixes, and a before-and-after report.
The goal is not theatrical hacking; it is secure engineering evidence that reduces real risk.

example.code: Threat model -> Validation -> Auth -> Authorization tests -> Secrets -> Dependencies -> Headers -> Logging -> Fixes
example.explanation: This sequence maps directly to the materials rule deliverables.

exercise.prompt: Create a secure-development checklist for one CertPath route.
exercise.starter:
Route:
Inputs:
Auth requirement:
Authorization rule:
Secret risk:
Log event:
Test:
exercise.answerGuide: Include validation, access control, test evidence, and any remediation needed.

quiz.prompt: Which artifact best proves secure-development practice?
options: A before-and-after security report with tests
quiz.explanation: The materials rule expects review evidence, fixes, and proof that the fix worked.

reference: Use the LFD121 course page and linked OpenSSF materials as official sources.
lessons.proofTask: Upload a before-and-after security review for one CertPath feature.

lessons.title: Authorized security assessment
lessons.summary: Define rules of engagement, scope, evidence, severity, remediation, and retest steps for systems you own or have permission to test.
learn: The materials file is explicit: only assess systems you own or have explicit permission to test.
A professional assessment starts with rules of engagement, scope, asset inventory, and safe reconnaissance.
Findings should include evidence, severity, remediation, retest results, and an executive summary.

example.code: Permission -> Scope -> Asset inventory -> Recon within scope -> Findings -> Remediation -> Retest -> Report
example.explanation: Permission and scope are part of the technical work, not paperwork afterthoughts.

exercise.prompt: Write rules of engagement for testing your own local app.
exercise.starter:
Authorized target:
Excluded targets:
Allowed methods:
Stop conditions:
Evidence handling:
exercise.answerGuide: Limit the work to owned assets, define excluded systems, and include safe stop conditions.

quiz.prompt: When is it acceptable to test a third-party public system?
quiz.explanation: The materials rule requires ownership or explicit permission.

reference: Use Cisco Skills for All course material and lab downloads only in authorized lab contexts.
lessons.proofTask: Upload rules of engagement and a safe assessment report for an owned or isolated lab target.

lessons.title: CertPath kickoff plan
lessons.summary: Use PMI KICKOFF to prepare both predictive and agile planning artifacts for CertPath work.
learn: The predictive kickoff artifact covers goal, scope, stakeholders, tasks, schedule, risks, communication, and definition of success.
The agile artifact covers product goal, user stories, prioritized backlog, sprint plan, review approach, retrospective, and definition of done.
The best learning evidence compares when each approach is useful.

example.code:
Predictive: scope -> tasks -> schedule
Agile: goal -> stories -> sprint -> review
example.explanation: Both approaches can plan CertPath work; the choice depends on uncertainty and iteration needs.

exercise.prompt: Write one predictive milestone and one agile user story for CertPath.
exercise.answerGuide: The milestone should be measurable; the user story should state user, need, and value.

quiz.prompt: Which item belongs in an agile kickoff?
quiz.explanation: Agile kickoff uses a product goal, stories, backlog, sprint plan, and review cycle.

reference: Use PMI KICKOFF and PMI free online courses as official sources.
lessons.proofTask: Upload one predictive kickoff and one agile kickoff for CertPath.

lessons.title: CertPath business model
lessons.summary: Model CertPath as a product using user segments, problem, value proposition, channels, costs, revenue options, competition, and adoption risk.
learn: Even if CertPath remains personal, the HP LIFE project asks you to understand its product logic.
A clear business model explains who the user is, what problem is painful, what value is offered, and what alternatives already exist.
The digital transformation proposal turns a manual process into a digital service with training, cost, benefit, risk, adoption, and KPI evidence.

example.code: User segment -> Problem -> Value proposition -> Channels -> Costs -> Revenue options -> Alternatives -> Risks
example.explanation: This keeps the idea concrete enough to evaluate.

exercise.starter:
For:
Who struggle with:
CertPath helps by:
Unlike:
exercise.answerGuide: Name a real learner segment, a specific pain, the benefit, and the alternative.

quiz.prompt: What makes a digital transformation proposal credible?
options: Clear current process, benefits, risks, adoption plan, and KPIs
quiz.explanation: Credible proposals explain process change, cost, benefit, risk, adoption, and measurement.

reference: Use HP LIFE digital business skills and course catalogue as official sources.
lessons.proofTask: Upload a CertPath business model and one digital transformation proposal.

lessons.title: Uganda MSME digital public infrastructure concept
lessons.summary: Design an inclusive DPI concept connecting identity, payments, consent, registry, data exchange, credit referrals, and services.
learn: The materials project asks for a Uganda MSME DPI concept that connects public and private services through trusted digital rails.
Strong DPI thinking covers public value, governance, inclusion, privacy, cybersecurity, interoperability, sustainability, and risk.
The service blueprint must support web, mobile, USSD, assisted channels, accessibility, grievance handling, and monitoring indicators.

example.code: Identity -> Consent -> Registry -> Data exchange -> Payments -> Credit referrals -> Service channels
example.explanation: This shows how users, institutions, and infrastructure connect.

exercise.prompt: Write one inclusive service blueprint row for an MSME applying for device financing.
exercise.starter:
Channel:
User action:
Back-office action:
Data exchanged:
Consent:
Support/grievance:
Metric:
exercise.answerGuide: Include access channel, consent, data flow, support path, and measurable indicator.

quiz.prompt: Which topic is essential in a DPI concept?
quiz.explanation: DPI requires governance, interoperability, inclusion, privacy, and security.

reference: Use ADBI E-Learning and the current DPI course catalogue as official sources.
lessons.proofTask: Upload a DPI concept note and inclusive service blueprint.

projects.title: Power Automate Partner Workflow
projects.businessProblem: Partner requests need validation, approval, reminders, notifications, and audit history.
projects.nextMilestone: Draft the request schema and approval states.

projects.title: Copilot Studio Study Coach
projects.businessProblem: Learners need a grounded assistant that answers from approved CertPath content and refuses unsupported exam-dump requests.
projects.nextMilestone: Define the allowed source set and refusal tests.

projects.title: AWS Educate Combined Cloud Portfolio
projects.businessProblem: A small application needs secure compute, networking, database, monitoring, and cost controls.
projects.nextMilestone: Create a one-page architecture for the combined AWS lab.

projects.title: CertPath MVP Plan
projects.businessProblem: CertPath needs a disciplined MVP plan with scope, milestones, risks, roles, and status reporting.
projects.nextMilestone: Write the first MVP milestone and risk log.

projects.title: CertPath Learner Research
projects.businessProblem: The app needs learner-centered improvements based on observed pain points and feedback.
projects.nextMilestone: Write three learner research questions.

projects.title: Secure CertPath or ProcureFlow
projects.businessProblem: The app needs a repeatable secure-development review and before-and-after remediation evidence.
projects.nextMilestone: Select one route and write its threat model.

projects.title: Authorised Security Assessment of Your Own App
projects.businessProblem: Security practice must stay inside owned or explicitly authorized systems with clear scope and evidence.
projects.nextMilestone: Write the rules of engagement for a local lab.

projects.title: Predictive and Agile CertPath Kickoff
projects.businessProblem: CertPath needs lightweight project-start artifacts for both predictable and iterative work.
projects.nextMilestone: Create one predictive milestone and three user stories.

projects.title: CertPath Business Model
projects.businessProblem: CertPath needs a product-style view of user value, alternatives, adoption risk, and operating model.
projects.nextMilestone: Draft the value proposition and user segment.

projects.title: Uganda MSME Digital Public Infrastructure Concept
projects.businessProblem: MSMEs need inclusive digital rails for identity, consent, payments, registries, data exchange, and service access.
projects.nextMilestone: Sketch the identity, consent, data exchange, and service channels.

projects.title: Real-Time Device-Financing Analytics
projects.businessProblem: Device-financing teams need near-real-time visibility into applications, approvals, repayments, and risk events.
projects.nextMilestone: Define the device-financing event schema.

projects.title: Azure Governance Lab
projects.businessProblem: Cloud resources need RBAC, policy, monitoring, budgets, and repeatable governance controls.
projects.nextMilestone: Draft the governance scope and role matrix.

projects.title: National MSME Digital Platform Architecture
projects.businessProblem: A national MSME platform needs scalable, secure, resilient, and governable cloud architecture.
projects.nextMilestone: Write the first architecture decision record.

projects.title: Multi-Region 10X Digital Activation Rollout
projects.businessProblem: A multi-region digital activation programme needs formal planning, governance, stakeholder communication, and risk control.
projects.nextMilestone: Create the rollout charter and stakeholder register.

projects.title: Audit of the 10X Data Ecosystem
projects.businessProblem: Programme data systems need audit evidence across governance, acquisition, operations, protection, and reporting integrity.
projects.nextMilestone: Define the audit objective and data-system scope.

projects.title: Cloud and Third-Party Risk Programme
projects.businessProblem: Cloud and vendor dependencies need risk identification, assessment, response, and monitoring.
projects.nextMilestone: List top cloud and vendor risk scenarios.

projects.title: Enterprise Architecture for Digital Procurement
projects.businessProblem: A digital procurement platform needs architecture views that connect business capability, applications, data, and technology.
projects.nextMilestone: Create the procurement capability map.

projects.title: Incident Response and Business Continuity Exercise
projects.businessProblem: A SaaS product needs tested incident response and continuity practices before a real outage or breach.
projects.nextMilestone: Write the tabletop incident scenario.

projects.title: Sourced Certification Research Agent
projects.businessProblem: Certification plans change, so CertPath needs a controlled research agent that produces sourced update reports without overwriting trusted content.
projects.nextMilestone: Define the official-source whitelist and report template.

projects.title: Explainable Document Classifier Prototype
projects.businessProblem: CertPath needs a small, explainable way to classify learning resources or support requests.
projects.nextMilestone: Create a small labeled dataset and baseline rules.

projects.title: Secure Software Development Pipeline
projects.businessProblem: CertPath needs repeatable CI/CD controls for dependency scanning, secret detection, tests, release approval, and vulnerability response.
projects.nextMilestone: Draft the pull-request security checklist.

TOGAF study guides and practice tests
